How to use Inkling for free?

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Steve A Johnson on UnsplashThe open-weight AI race has a serious new contender.
Just a few months after launching Thinking Machines Lab, Mira Murati and her team have introduced Inkling, their first foundation model. Instead of chasing a single benchmark, Inkling focuses on becoming a balanced multimodal model that developers can customize, fine-tune, and deploy.
The model comes with 975 billion total parameters, 41 billion active parameters, a massive 1 million token context window, and native support for text, images, and audio.
More importantly, Thinking Machines has released the full model weights, making Inkling one of the largest open-weight foundation models available today.Let’s explore what makes Inkling interesting and how it compares with today’s leading AI models.

What is Inkling?
Inkling is a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) transformer designed from scratch by Thinking Machines.
Some of its key specifications include:

• 975B total parameters

• 41B active parameters

• 1M token context window

• Trained on 45 trillion multimodal tokens

• Native text, image and audio reasoning

• Open weights

• Fine-tuning available through Tinker

• Adjustable reasoning effort for balancing speed and quality
Unlike many recent releases that optimize almost entirely for coding or reasoning, Inkling aims to be a general-purpose foundation model suitable for building customized AI applications.

Built for Customization Instead of Just Benchmarks
One interesting decision from Thinking Machines is that they openly acknowledge Inkling is not the strongest overall model.
Instead, their goal is to create a model that developers can easily adapt through fine-tuning.According to the company, the combination of:

• multimodal understanding

• efficient reasoning

• controllable thinking

• open weights

• Tinker fine-tuning platform
makes Inkling an ideal starting point for custom enterprise AI systems instead of treating it as a fixed chatbot.

Model Architecture
Inkling uses a modern MoE architecture with several optimizations. Some notable technical details include:

• 256 routed experts per MoE layer

• 2 shared experts

• 6 experts activated per token

• Relative positional embeddings instead of RoPE

• Sliding window + global attention

• Native multimodal processing

• Encoder-free image and audio pipeline
The model was trained on NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 systems using a combination of Muon and Adam optimizers.

Benchmark Performance
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Inkling performs surprisingly well across a wide variety of tasks, although it doesn’t always claim first place.
Reasoning
Inkling scores:

• 29.7% on Humanity’s Last Exam (text only)

• 46.0% with tools

• 97.1% on AIME 2026

• 87.2% on GPQA Diamond
While impressive, frontier closed models still lead in pure reasoning.
For example:

• Claude Fable 5 reaches 53.3% on HLE

• GPT 5.6 Sol reaches 47.2%

• Gemini 3.1 Pro scores 44.7%

• GLM 5.2 achieves 40.1%
Inkling remains competitive but isn’t the top reasoning model.

Coding Performance
Coding is where Inkling becomes much more competitive.
It achieves:

• 77.6% SWEBench Verified

• 54.3% SWEBench Pro

• 63.8 Terminal Bench 2.1

• 1233 GDPVal-AA v2
Claude Fable 5 still dominates software engineering with:

• 95.0% SWEBench Verified

• 80.0% SWEBench Pro

• 84.6 Terminal Bench
However, Inkling clearly outperforms most previous open-weight models including Nemotron 3 Ultra and Kimi K2.5.

Agent Performance
Inkling also performs well in AI agent evaluations.
It records:

• 74.1% MCP Atlas

• 23.7% Tau 3 Banking
GLM 5.2 performs slightly better on MCP Atlas at 77.8%, while Claude Fable 5 reaches 83.3%. This suggests Inkling is already capable of powering sophisticated autonomous agents.

Multimodal Capabilities
Unlike many open models, Inkling was trained natively on multiple modalities. Vision benchmarks include:

• MMMU Pro: 73.3%

• CharXiv RQ: 78.1%

• CharXiv RQ with Python: 82.0%
Audio benchmarks include:

• Audio MC: 56.6%

• MMAU: 77.2%

• VoiceBench: 91.4%
Gemini 3.1 Pro still leads most multimodal evaluations, but Inkling delivers strong performance for an open-weight release.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Adjustable Thinking Effort
One of Inkling’s most unique features is controllable thinking effort.
Developers can increase or decrease reasoning depth depending on latency and cost requirements. Instead of always generating long reasoning chains, Inkling dynamically adjusts its token usage.
Thinking Machines reports that Inkling can reach similar performance to Nemotron 3 Ultra on Terminal Bench while using roughly one-third of the generated tokens, making it significantly more efficient for production workloads.

Strong Safety for an Open Model
Safety has historically been a weak area for open-weight models. Inkling performs surprisingly well. It scores:

• 78.0% on FORTRESS Adversarial

• 95.9% on FORTRESS Benign

• 98.6% on StrongREJECT
According to Thinking Machines, Inkling achieves the strongest FORTRESS Adversarial performance among the open-weight models they compared, indicating better handling of harmful requests without excessively refusing benign ones.

Inkling-Small is Also Coming
Thinking Machines also previewed Inkling-Small.Specifications include:

• 276B parameters

• 12B active parameters
Interestingly, Inkling-Small matches or even exceeds the larger model on several benchmarks, making it an attractive option for developers who prioritize lower inference costs and latency.

Availability
Inkling is available immediately through:

• Tinker

• Hugging Face

• Together AI

• Fireworks AI

• Modal

• Databricks

• Baseten

• vLLM

• llama.cpp

• Transformers ecosystem
Developers can also fine-tune the model directly on the Tinker platform.

Final Thoughts
Inkling is not trying to beat every frontier model on every benchmark, and Thinking Machines is transparent about that.
Instead, it delivers something arguably more valuable for developers: a large, open-weight multimodal foundation model that combines competitive reasoning, solid coding ability, native audio and vision support, a 1M-token context window, and controllable thinking efficiency. While models like Claude Fable 5, GPT 5.6 Sol, and Gemini 3.1 Pro still lead several benchmark categories, Inkling narrows the gap while giving developers the freedom to inspect, fine-tune, and deploy the model on their own infrastructure.
For teams building custom AI products rather than relying solely on closed APIs, Inkling is one of the most significant open-weight releases of 2026 and sets a strong foundation for the broader Thinking Machines model family.
